title: Inflation to add billions to spending
full_text: Other Editions 04 May 2026 News Inflation to add billions to spending The Australian Financial Review Ronald Mizen | Political correspondent 30 April 2026 968 words English AFNR 1 Copyright 2026. Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited. Treasurer Jim Chalmers is preparing to unveil a multibillion-dollar blowout in the May budget in spending programs linked to inflation, such as the aged pension, after annual price rises surged to a two-year high. Annual inflation leapt from 3.7 per cent to 4.6 per cent in March as the Middle East conflict sent prices soaring for key commodities such as crude, petrol and diesel. The surge in inflation cemented expectations that the Reserve Bank will raise rates for the third time this year when it meets next week. Chalmers said the rising cost of fuel had added about 0.8 percentage points to headline inflation, warning that this was yet to peak. Treasury has forecast inflation to be above 5 per cent by the middle of the year. "In the coming months, we expect the impact of this oil shock to be felt more broadly across prices and across the economy," he said. The budget is expected to show that spending on inflation-linked programs, which include the $67 billion aged pension and veterans support payments, will be about $4 billion higher than forecast in the December mid-year update, according to analysis by economist Chris Richardson. He said spending on these programs would be $1.9 billion higher this financial year and $2.3 billion higher in 2026-27. The increased cost of inflation-linked programs could push expected new spending beyond $20 billion in the budget, due on May 12. The government has already announced $14 billion in extra defence spending and there is the $2.55 billion cost of halving the fuel excise for three months. The spending blowout will wipe out most of the $22 billion in net savings the government expects to make from slashing the growth rate of the National Disability Insurance Scheme from 10 per cent to an average of 2 per cent over the next four years. The treasurer has promised a reform budget, but is grappling with resurgent inflation, a decade of deficits, record spending and public debt soon to surpass $1 trillion. Commonwealth Bank chief economist Luke Yeaman - who was until last year a deputy secretary at Treasury - said Chalmers' budget would "be judged on how much new spending it contains". He questioned the size of the savings the government expected to bank from overhauling the NDIS, suggesting that a more realistic figure was $8 billion. "Achieving average NDIS cost growth of 2 per cent over four years looks highly ambitious and unlikely, creating upside risk to the budget estimates," Yeaman said in a note to clients on Wednesday. "Getting average cost growth down to 4-5 per cent over that period is more realistic and would still be a major achievement, but that would now punch a $14 billion hole in the budget out to 2029-30. Yeaman said Chalmers would also probably capitulate to demands from the states for more federal funding, "which will eat into the savings". Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has flagged a fuel security resilience package in the budget, which is likely to include billions of dollars to boost Australia's fuel reserves. The government has already announced that it will underwrite purchases of fuel and fertiliser on the international spot market. CBA does expect the government to book a small revenue bump in the near-term from changes to the capital gains tax discount and negative gearing. The country's largest bank said higher commodity prices and elevated inflation would have a positive effect on the four-year budget bottomline, which Richardson expects to total an extra $27 billion in revenue. But Chalmers has played down expectations that the war will deliver a significant revenue windfall. "We don't currently expect the sort of revenue bump that you have seen speculated about," he said last week. "In fact, if we take the impacts of this war in the Middle East on the revenue side, it's possible if we were finalising those forecasts today that, in some years, the revenue impacts would be negative rather than positive." A multibillion-dollar blowout in Labor's home battery scheme is also expected, at least in the short term. The scheme was initially forecast to cost $2.3 billion over four years, but this was increased to $7.2 billion in the mid-year update due to strong demand. However $3.3 billion has already been spent in the first 10 months of the program, nearly half of the total budget. With demand for electric vehicles surging after the United States and Israel attacked Iran on February 28, upward revisions are also expected to Labor's tax exemption for EVs unless access to the policy is curtailed. The government is also expected to spend more money to extend a temporary two-year 15 per cent pay rise for childcare workers, which expires in November. Albanese has flagged the potential for more cost-of-living relief to offset what Richardson said would be a $12 billion hit to households in 2026-27 from inflation being higher than wage growth. Richardson said the fuel tax cut and increases to inflation-linked payments would offset what is likely to be a $6 billion to $7 billion hit this year, but next year was looking more difficult for households. "Politics says fill that hole. Economics says if you're going to fill that hole, at the very least make other savings that mean you're not adding to demand in a way that makes the Reserve Bank's life harder," Richardson said. Yeaman said CBA was expecting the government to deliver some additional cost of living relief in the budget, "but we expect the government to resist the urge to make a big splash." summary: Treasurer Jim Chalmers is set to announce a significant increase in spending linked to inflation in the upcoming budget, with inflation rising to 4.6% in March. The expected spending on inflation-linked programs, including the aged pension, is projected to exceed $20 billion, largely due to higher costs from the recent oil price surge. This spending increase may offset anticipated savings from reforms to the National Disability Insurance Scheme.
publication:
Chalmers talks a big game - it's time to act on intergenerational fairness
The Age02 May 20261,330 wordsMillie Muroi

When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers "entitled" for wanting more.
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Inflation has risen to 4.6%, prompting a multibillion-dollar spending increase in the budget.
Spending on inflation-linked programs is expected to be $4 billion higher than previous forecasts.
The government faces challenges in balancing new spending with budget savings from NDIS reforms.
relevance: Understanding the government's fiscal strategies and inflation impacts is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals in assessing economic risks and opportunities.
